preamble: The transformation from the voiced alveolar affricate /dz/ to the voiceless dental fricative /θ/ is a historically attested sound change, notably occurring in the evolution of Old Spanish. This shift involves a series of phonetic processes including deaffrication, devoicing, and a slight dentalization. Old Spanish /ts/ and /dz/ deaffricated in the Late Middle Ages. Menéndez Pidal (1987) noted that by the early sixteenth century, the interdental, fricative pronunciations /θ/ and /ð/ were becoming general, with the two sounds merging into a single voiceless one in the 17th century.
phoneticEffects: Deaffrication (affricate to fricative), Devoicing (voiced to voiceless), Dentalization (alveolar to dental)

examples.note: The Old Spanish voiced alveolar affricate /dz/ (spelled 'z') first deaffricated to a voiced dental fricative /ð/, which later devoiced and merged with /θ/ (from Old Spanish /ts/) in Castilian Spanish.

sources: The Genesis of Spanish /θ/: A Revised Model - MDPI (July 22 2022)
